acara_description: Plan and conduct investigations involving discrete and continuous numerical data; analyse and report findings.

default: Primary = first-hand.
You collect it directly through surveys, experiments, or observation.
Secondary data is collected by others.

questions.worked_solution: Primary data is data you collect yourself through surveys, experiments or observation.

options: Data collected by conducting a survey
Data already collected by someone else

answer.correct: Data already collected by someone else

default: Secondary = second-hand.
You obtain it from books, databases, websites or other sources.
Secondary data exists before your investigation.

questions.worked_solution: Secondary data: already collected by others — you analyse it for your purpose.

questions.stem: Which of the following is a CONTINUOUS variable?

default: Continuous variables can take any value within a range.
Height can be measured to any precision: 162.3 cm, 162.31 cm, etc.
Number of pets can only be whole numbers (discrete).

questions.worked_solution: Height is continuous — it can take any value within a range, not just whole numbers.

questions.stem: Which of the following is a DISCRETE variable?
options: Number of students in a class

answer.correct: Number of students in a class

default: Discrete variables can only take specific, separate values.
You can have 25 or 26 students, but not 25.5 students.
Temperature and mass are continuous.

questions.worked_solution: Number of students is discrete — only whole number values are possible.

questions.stem: Which is the most appropriate display for the heights (in cm) of 30 students?

default: Heights are numerical and continuous. Stem-and-leaf works well for ~30 data points.
Dot plots are good but can be crowded with 30 values.
Pie charts are for parts of a whole; bar charts are for categories.

questions.worked_solution: Stem-and-leaf plot suits 30 numerical values and retains each data point.

default: A census collects data from every member of the group of interest.
For example, the Australian census asks every household in Australia.
A sample survey only asks a subset.

questions.worked_solution: A census collects data from the entire population, not just a sample.

questions.stem: Which sampling method gives every person an equal chance of being selected?

default: In simple random sampling, names or numbers are selected randomly.
For example, putting all names in a hat and drawing.
Every person has the same chance of being picked.

questions.worked_solution: Simple random sampling: each member of the population has an equal chance of being selected.

questions.stem: A student wants to investigate 'How many hours per week do Year 7 students spend on social media?'. What type of data is this?

default: Time in whole hours: 0, 1, 2, 3, ... — countable.
If measured in exact fractions of hours it could be continuous, but 'hours' typically means discrete.
Discrete numerical.

questions.worked_solution: Hours per week (counted in whole hours) is discrete numerical data.

questions.stem: A student takes a sample of 10 students from a school of 500. What fraction of the school is sampled?

default: Fraction = \(\frac{\text{sample size}}{\text{population size}}\).

questions.stem: An investigation conclusion states: 'Year 7 students who play sport get higher grades.' What is a limitation of this claim?

default: A relationship between two variables doesn't mean one causes the other.
There may be a third variable (e.g., general health) affecting both.
Correlation ≠ causation.

questions.worked_solution: Even if sport and grades are related, sport doesn't necessarily cause higher grades — correlation ≠ causation.
